mainEntity.name: What types of fireplace inserts do you install?

acceptedAnswer.text: We install three primary types of fireplace inserts, each suited to different homes and lifestyles: Wood-burning inserts are ideal for homeowners with a working chimney who love the authentic crackle, aroma, and radiant heat of a traditional fire — they deliver high heat output and work well during power outages. Gas inserts are convenient, clean-burning, and easy to operate with a switch or remote, available in natural gas or propane with realistic ceramic logs and adjustable flame settings. Pellet inserts are an eco-friendly option that burns compressed wood pellets with minimal ash and consistent heat output, great for homeowners who want efficiency without splitting firewood. During your in-home consultation, we evaluate your chimney, venting, fuel preferences, and heating goals before recommending the best fit.

mainEntity.name: Can a fireplace insert fit into my existing fireplace?

acceptedAnswer.text: In most cases, yes — but a proper fit depends on several factors we evaluate during inspection. Firebox dimensions (height, width, and depth) must accommodate the insert with proper clearances. The chimney condition matters — the flue, liner, and masonry must be safe and structurally sound, or repaired before installation. Flue size and venting are important since most inserts require a stainless-steel liner sized to the appliance for safe draft and efficiency. The hearth and surround must meet code clearances to combustible materials. If your chimney needs relining, tuckpointing, or a new cap before installation, we'll explain the work clearly and can complete everything in-house so you only deal with one trusted team.

mainEntity.name: Can I convert a wood fireplace to a gas fireplace insert?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — converting a wood-burning fireplace to a gas insert is one of the most popular upgrades we perform for Columbus homeowners. It's a great way to keep the look of a real fireplace while gaining the convenience and efficiency of gas. A typical conversion involves a chimney evaluation to inspect the existing flue, masonry, and damper to confirm the system can safely accommodate a gas appliance; gas line installation by a licensed technician running a natural gas or propane line to the firebox with code-compliant shutoffs; a stainless-steel liner properly sized for safe venting and optimal draft; and insert selection where we help you choose a gas insert with the flame style, log set, and heat output that fit your home. Once converted, you'll enjoy push-button operation, cleaner burning, and consistent heat — without hauling firewood or cleaning ash.

Most fireplace insert installations are completed in a single day. A typical project includes a pre-installation chimney inspection to confirm the system is safe and ready; any necessary repairs or relining, including stainless-steel liner installation sized to the insert; insert placement and connection with proper clearances and venting hookups; gas line connection (for gas inserts) by a certified technician; and final testing and a walkthrough so you know exactly how to operate and maintain your new insert. Larger projects involving extensive masonry work or custom surrounds may take longer. We'll provide a clear timeline during your consultation so there are no surprises on installation day.

mainEntity.name: Can I install a fireplace insert myself?

acceptedAnswer.text: No — we strongly recommend leaving fireplace insert installation to a certified professional. While inserts may look straightforward, improper installation can create serious safety hazards and void your warranty. DIY installation is risky because venting and draft must be precisely sized and sealed (incorrect liner sizing can cause carbon monoxide leaks or chimney fires); gas line work requires a licensed technician and must meet local code, and unpermitted work can result in fines and insurance issues; clearance to combustibles must follow manufacturer specs exactly to prevent house fires; manufacturer warranties typically require professional installation, and DIY work voids coverage on most premium inserts; and local permits and inspections are often required and can be difficult for homeowners to navigate. Our CSIA-certified technicians handle every aspect — from inspection and liner installation to gas hookup and final testing — so your insert operates safely, efficiently, and within warranty for years to come.
